speaker: interviewer
text: 1996, and we're in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Yeah, I'm all right. Thank you, Susie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, you were telling us about the landing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were bodies around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were shooting at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: People around you were dying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, had been killed. I mean, when we landed, mostly first the group, the initial landing parties were well-- not well inland. But they were already a few kilometers inland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we landed-- which was about 8:00 AM, I think-- the main front of the fighting, of the capturing of the shore, you know, trying to make a bridgehead, was a-- a little ahead of me, a little ahead of us, or our particular group. So we saw the results of the first landing. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, I think the Canadians landed the same place as us. I think it was the second Canadian Army landed at the same time as the British troops. So you just saw them, the Canadian and British troops, laying in the sand, dead, or being washed up by the sea and against the beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: You landed safely?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your bulldozer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went into the first town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a place called Ver-sur-Mer. It was a small village.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first you had go on the beach. You had to land on the beach. And you had to take off all your waterproofing equipment, which was on the bulldozer. Most of the tanks were all waterproofed. So you had to prepare your equipment to go forward.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you under fire while you did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only when we were coming off the LST. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, things are a little vague, you know? A little-- but I remember that once we did land and took the waterproofing equipment off the bulldozers and the equipment, I think we rested a while. I don't know how long for. It couldn't have been very, very long. Then we moved forward, going towards Bayeux.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went into the village first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the-- from Arromanches, from beach Arromanches, we went into this place called Ver-sur-Mer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there civilians around?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were all the civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Might've escaped, run away. I don't know. Do you know, first time I ever saw a civilian, when we went into Bayeux. But this little place, Ver-sur-Mer, was picked out as one of the places where we had to build an airfield. And I think the second airfield--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second airfield in Normandy was built at this place called Ver-sur-Mer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you involved with the building of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there for? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't worry. One or two days, that's all. Two or three days. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you lodged in-- in camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we were dug in, slit trenches. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first days, no, fair. I remember it was summer there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had your own rations with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only field rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you ate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that what you ate?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah. Because the field kitchens and the supplied food didn't come in till about the third day-- no-- the third day after-- after the invasion started. So we were living, literally, just on what we called field [? tack-- ?] field rations. I think I-- we had our first bread about the forth day after we landed, [LAUGHS] fourth or fifth day after we landed. Where did you go after that? You went to Bayeux?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went through to Bayeux. We didn't stay there. We went into Bayeux. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we moved to a place called [INAUDIBLE] Shores.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there? That's still all in Normandy, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes, still Normandy. When it is? So they'd only just been captured. And then we-- we had to dig in a place called Jerusalem [INAUDIBLE]. And I actually got wounded there, in my leg, by mortar fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that during a battle? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were being bombarded by the Germans. Because they were fighting very, very strongly there at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still didn't see civilians at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we'd seen some civilians, like in Bayeux. In this place, Jerusalem, where we were dug in, it was a farming area. And we did see some farmers there. You know? Some French people there. And-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your unit ever take prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your unit ever take prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: They could have done. I don't know. I never took a German prisoner myself. But I saw plenty of them being-- being taken back to the beaches.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fair. Yeah, never-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think we ever abused German prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many battles did you actually go through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were all battles. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any major battles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Battle of Caen. We were dug in a place called Carpiquet, which was an airfield, which we tried to capture. Because we-- we needed a airfield. But unfortunately, the Germans were dug in very, very strong there. They had one of their SS regiments there, and they're defending this town, which was a main town to sort of break out of Normandy. So I was in this Battle of Carpiquet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with the-- how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a big deal, scared, frightened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just kept my fingers crossed, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were a soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an enlisted man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. But I mean, being a soldier doesn't necessarily mean you're always in the infantry. I wasn't in infantry. I was in the field regiment. And we were always close to the infantry. Normally they would be ahead of us. But obviously, you got involved in fighting. If you were-- it was very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your friends get killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had two of my friends get killed. In-- one in Jerusalem with me. And one in Carpiquet. There was a lot that was killed. But there were these two particular friends I had. Funnily enough, one of them was called Max [? Bygraves. ?] Not the-- the actor. One of them called--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were buried in Jerusalem, at this place in Jerusalem. It was a very, very small place. I think you'd have a difficultly finding it on the map. Very, very small village, which was either north or south of Tilly-sur-Seulles. I can't remember now. And I lost a friend at Carpiquet as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever come in contact with German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Oh yeah, not actual fighting. But I certainly came in contact with them. I saw plenty of them dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from being dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the ones that were captured. I never got involved in hand-to-hand fighting. That was a World War I system of fighting. It was never that kind of fighting. Maybe except for on special certain occasions like Battle of Arnhem, where they engaged in hand-to-hand fighting. But there was very little hand-to-hand fighting in the part of Normandy I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did meet civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you? How did you treat them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I dare not talk about the French people. I didn't particularly like 'em. They-- I mean, a lot of them had got married to German soldiers. They had built families together there during the whole occupy-- occupied by German-- by Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of them had got married, made-- made up families there. And that actually seemed to be a little indifferent. There were a few-- obviously a few people [INAUDIBLE] sold out and things like that. You know? But didn't see much of them. I mean, it just involved mainly, the whole time, moving, moving, moving. Did you have orders regarding civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only time we orders we got was when we went into Germany. And there was no fraternization. That was after the war finished, where we couldn't fraternize.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in France, you had no orders about civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Per se, we didn't be in contact with them all that much. I mean, most places we went through had been captured, had been destroyed, the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, all I saw was destroyed villages. You know, say, well, I've seen the population has probably went away somewhere. You know? Try and move somewhere else. But most of the time all we saw was destroyed villages. Michael, does any incident stick out in your mind during this period? Something memorable that may have happened that you may recall just now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think there was too much turmoil. And I mean, yeah, there's some things that happened and you can't recollect how they happened and why it happened. Is there anything you could think of? Do you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange enough, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recollect seeing any children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- yeah, women, yeah. But I think most of the population, they'd evacuated. I mean, there was a big battle going on around them. I don't think they'd stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go from Normandy now. From Normandy, where did you go? Where did your-- you went into Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went into Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Leon, ?] Ghent. It's called Gand, G-A-N-D. But it's spelled G-H-E-N-T, Ghent. A little place south of Brussels that we were dug in. But I can't remember the name. It was an airfield quite close to there. I think it was the first time I had some RR, rest and recreation, was in Ghent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went into the town, drank, then went back to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet civilians then, in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. As a matter of fact, I met a Belgian doctor. He was a Jewish doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to this day, I can't remember his name. But what I do remember, he had a little daughter who had a crippled arm. But say, you-- these things are momentary, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: You remember a few things, you see them, and you go on your way. I mean, probably a lot of events happened that I can't even recollect today. Only the most important ones, you know, you can recollect.

speaker: interviewer
text: And each of these places that you were at, each of these towns, there was a battle ensuing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, all had been captured. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were cleaning up or preparing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, cleaning up, mainly building roads, rebuilding roads, rebuilding airfields if they needed rebuilding. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mhm. That-- that was the main function of the sort of regiment I was in at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Ghent, or in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a couple of days, few days. Then we moved on, I think, into Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: First place I remember in Holland was actually Tilburg, that we'd captured. Captured the same day that we entered it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the German forces?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd sort of run off, gone somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: No prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: No German prisoners taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see any. I didn't see any. There could have been. But I didn't see. You don't-- you don't always see these things in the army. There's always special units that are given this kind of job, you know, moving prisoners to the-- to the rear of the lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Tilburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Tilburg, we stayed there for two or three days. But the second day or first day we were there, we were given a-- what they call a company order, where they wanted volunteers, or Jewish volunteers. And there was only two Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] in my unit. There was myself and a fellow called Dave Goldstein. And apparently the small local shul in Tilburg had been desecrated by the Germans. So they asked for volunteers to help clean up this little shul there, or synagogue. But there was quite a few of us, even, like, non-Jewish soldiers who

speaker: interviewer
text: Volunteered--

speaker: interviewee
text: --volunteered to do the job. And it's whilst we were at this shul, in this place cleaning up, that I met two families. One was called Esse. And one was called [? Pollack. ?] [? Pollack ?] was the cousins of this family, Esse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances of meeting them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in with-- we met them whilst we were cleaning up this shul. And of course, they were some of the Jewish families that were left behind there, who were also clean-- cleaning up the shul there, cleaning up the synagogue there. And I got to know these people. I spoke to them. I took them food. As a matter of fact, I've still got-- found some letters that they wrote to my wife, thanking me, you know, thanking us for supplying them with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, they were from Tilburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I still remember the address. They-- they lived at a place called 23 [INAUDIBLE], in Tilburg. I don't know what his occupation was. I think he might have been a merchant or something. And his wife, [? Yeta, ?] they didn't have any children at that time. And I don't know if they were hidden. I-- I-- I was told that they managed to hide themselves in somewhere in Tilburg, with them and the other family, [? Pollack. ?] And I think after three days we left. And they communicated with me, following up. They wrote letters to me whilst I was still in Holland, or whilst I was still in the forces. And they did write a couple of letters to my wife here in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the other Jewish families of Tilburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was a few. But they were the only two families I remember, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or who I communicated with.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't know what happened to the rest of the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- yet-- oh, incidentally, they-- William Esse, a man, actually gave me his yellow Star of David, which I donated to the Jewish museum here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, I don't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you're not in a place long enough, you know, to assess what's happened or what is happening. You know? You're just move the whole-- just on the move the whole time. And you think that the Esses and the [? Pollacks ?] had been hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, were in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't mention to you with whom or how?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just said, they'd managed to sort of keep away from the Germans, who were occupied in Tilburg at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: They must've been happy to see you boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, of course they were. Yeah, most of the Dutch people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were your-- your-- the British army treated by civilians that you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. Good. We always treated them with courtesy and with care, and with a bit of pity as well, what had happened there. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were treated well by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was treated well, by what? The British?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, by the civilians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh yeah. Oh yeah. When we came as liberators, and I don't think they could've treated us otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food and gifts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't have any food. We gave them food. I gave them food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the food from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From [LAUGHS] the canteen. And I had a sergeant friend who-- from the kitchen. You know? I managed to get some food. Of course, I had my own food as well, my rations, which I gave to them. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they like the best out of your rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The chocolate, I think. [LAUGHS] The chocolate. The-- yeah, I think chocolate mainly. And then they always asked for cigarettes, of course. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained friendly with the two-- with the Esses after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after the-- [? when ?] I came back to England, yeah, communicated with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period, did you hear of any propaganda at all that was going, spreading through Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, soldiers were-- can't explain. Maybe civilians do, but soldiers very rarely hear these things. Mainly because you're on the move the whole time. And your main purpose, I suppose, is you tried to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you went into Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we entered Germany from a place called Xanten or Wesel. And I think the first place-- I was trying to remember what it was. Oh yeah, funnily enough, [INAUDIBLE] going into Germany, we-- on our way from Xanten, we came to a place called Kleve. Kleve. And from there I was attached to the American Ninth Army. Not me personally, but the camp that I was in was attached to the American Ninth Army for two or three weeks. And we were the American Ninth Army then. And the main purpose of this was to learn their methods of making airfields, repairing airfields, and repairing roads. So we were testing them as-- on a [? sort ?] [? of ?] training course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was their method different to yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were more efficient. And they'd done it quicker, and they had the equipment. We didn't. Mostly equipment, you know? I mean, they had things for making roads which we didn't have, and the American army-- or the Americans supplied the British army with. So their methods were a lot more efficient than ours and quicker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish boys in those units?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I met a few Jewish boys in the American army. Yeah. And I won't say too much about the American Army. Because maybe I had three weeks with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I won't say too much about the American army. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so we've gone from Kleve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I went to Kleve, [INAUDIBLE] to Xanten, [INAUDIBLE]. Then from there, let's see, I went to Munster. A town called Munster. I think we only passed through there. And then there we went to another place called Rain. And I did pick up some artifacts there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of artifacts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I picked up-- it was lying in the road, or lying somewhere. It was a Hitlerjugend [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] membership form. And I picked up some medals there. I picked up some medals from some dead German soldiers, which I gave to the Jewish Museum. And then from there, there were-- this-- this is-- the only thing that's a little vague with me at the moment, it was either from Munster or Brunswick that we went to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, before we get to that, let's go back to Munster.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- was it a large town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Munster was a large town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your function there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's the army function there?

speaker: interviewer
text: But your function?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're just going through there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your function?

speaker: interviewee
text: My particular function was nothing. We just passed through there, at Munster. I think I stayed-- we stayed there for probably a day. The soldiers who were on the move sometimes didn't always have something to do. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you with the American forces?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were you separated by the state?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was only with the American forces for about two or three weeks, with the American Ninth Army. And after-- I left the American Ninth. I went back to our-- not regiment-- to our division, that we went onto Munster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who your commanding officer was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No idea. No. That's a very difficult thing--

speaker: interviewer
text: No names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you knew you were heading south?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must've been. Of course, I think after that, we went on to Brunswick or Hannover.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in each of these places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Days, you know? Not any length of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told where you were going each time? Each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a map?

speaker: interviewee
text: The officers had maps. But not the ordinary sap or private soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you were in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, of course we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that there were concentration camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we'd heard about them. Of course we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you heard? As a soldier, what had you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you've got to remember, we had army newspapers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had American newspapers, army newspapers. And of course, we read it from there. And of course the mere fact that I heard from my friends, the Esses, we'd learned-- we'd learned quite a bit. You know, what happened to the Jewish population, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew what was going on in the rest of Europe, in Poland and all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Yeah. Well, after were-- we were already aware of the concentration camps. We were aware of them and what the Germans were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the non-Jewish soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they felt the same as I did. We felt, obviously, quite a lot of hatred to the Germans, especially what they'd done in France and Belgium. You know? So we'd heard all about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you got letter from home, from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they mention anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I've still got letters form my wife and all that. And funnily enough, it wasn't mentioned. Why, I don't know. Maybe because of the censors. I don't know. Because your mail was censored at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you write home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But of course, I-- you couldn't always write. You weren't allowed to say where you were. All you could write, say, whether you were fit and well. You know, and it was very difficult. [INAUDIBLE] some-- some letters were terribly censored. I remember I once wrote a letter to my wife. After I came back from the army, I got demobilized from the army. She showed me this letter. About three quarters of it was cut out. And I can't remember if I'd ever mentioned anything that you could call security reasons. But it was censored, very heavily censored. Which makes sense, in any case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, I'd like to talk about, now, entering into Bergen-Belsen. Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember they said that we were going into one of the camps. And I think at the time they said, we're going to a place called Bergen-Belsen, which we didn't attach the Belsen. And then we heard [INAUDIBLE] again, was Bergen on the Hill, or Belsen, or Bergen on the Hill, or Belsen on the Hill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard of the town name before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We-- we had heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that it was a concentration camp. We did hear that. And that-- well, all the things that you could hear about a concentration camp, I suppose we heard. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But a soldier's attitude, Susie, is not of disbelief. Of-- you had a job to do. You know? You had-- you had a job to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you'd have to go ahead and do it, irrespective of the circumstances or what was going on. But I suppose as a Jewish soldier, I had great feelings of qualm and uneasiness. You know, I thought I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can't actually explain-- describe my feelings, not at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's only when I actually got into the camp, that after, I felt-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] disbelief. You know? What I saw. Had the military prepared you for what you might see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they told us it was a concentration camp. They told us it was notorious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you what you might see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They said probably what you'll see may shock you. But we were there to clean up the place, clear up, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had British army been in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were. We were-- I-- I went in there with my field regiment, I think a day before it was actually-- day after the first British troops had got in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's April?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, April. I think the end of April or May. I can't remember. I think it must have been any time between April and May, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, I'd like you to describe the actual going into the camp. Can you tell us what you saw? How did you go there, by foot or by--

speaker: interviewee
text: By truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, with my-- with my company, field regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about what you saw as you approached Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember going through a big gate. And you could see a lot of smoke. You could smell the place. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But we entered the place, it was just a shambles, people laying dead or half dead. I remember seeing some great big pits that had been dug by the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think just disbelief, actually-- hard to describe, Susie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, the army had been in already. The British army had already entered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think we were the second. We might've been the first. I don't even know, Susie. I'll be quite honest. We might have been the first British troops in there. But when we were there, British troops were already in there. So we might've just been on the tail end, just behind them. I don't know. Of course, movements were very, very hard to distinguish in those days, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say you were on a move. You weren't always told everything. You know, where you're going and what were you gonna do. Was there a sign up that said this is Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I can't remember. Too far back. But I know we went through some two big gates though. So I remember the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there soldiers still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, German?

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans still around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I think they'd already been held captive. So we might've just been on the second line that went in there, the second body of British soldiers that went in there. I do-- do remember, when they brought out Josef Kramer and Irma Grese, and the other lady wardens. And there was some men wardens at-- I just caught glimpses of them as they were brought out. From there, I don't know where they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they being treated? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in a minute. Thank you. Michael, we'll continue in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape two of the interview with Michael Sheridan. It's the 15th of April, 1996. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
